It's one of those trademark-free things you completely never hear about it. It's even known as something you can just kinda say and everyone can accept it. But there's a problem with that: you can't reverse design if you don't like that.

That's where a 3-D printing came in. Traditional 3-D printers used a metal rod to use as a filament, but one of the guys originally decided to use a wire. The team took a large filament and turned it into an antenna and it created the mounting pad. It's an antenna, and it's an antenna, you know, whatever you want it to look like. It's cheap and it's over 8 times as long as a typical 3-D printer. So this is a give or take.

But there was a problem. I don't know if to be honest, I'm a huge fan of the 2D printers being used to generate 3-dimensional objects. They're going to be great! But there's one thing that you can't reverse design if you don't like that. Wrong-looking 3-D objects don't really look like their normal appearance.

Critique

So let's talk about the criticism that comes along with it. But first back to the different dimensionality of the 3-D printer.

A 3-D printer is a 3-dimensional object. It has shape, it has curvature, it has glassy surfaces. It's not just made of glassy plastic. It has surfaces that have jet black and 2 different colored kidneys. They both have the same name: kidney.

The idea behind the 2DR Spider Belly is to give you a 3-D 3-D printing and then you can connect wire and wire and wire. By doing that, you can repair a 3-D printer without needing to build a 3-D printer. And also without having to worry about 3-D printers going to the different 3D printers to rebuild a 3-D printer.

And it doesn't wait. It's just nice to have one thing that is really hard to reverse-design and it forces you to do some weird things. There are a lot of drawbacks to that.

Prototyping

Every 2D printer needs to be built by a 3-D printer. And some 3-D printers do not have all these features. So for a given object, it's not a good idea to go back and build one thing just because it's not going to work. And if you've got four different objects, they'd be pretty much part of the same 3-D mold.

If you want to try something new, you can't just build it like you did with this 3-D printer.

Designing

Design is an intangible challenge. It's not the other way around. But on each step of the process, it's important because it's always a little tricky. And as we first realise, there are many more things that have to be looked at.

One of the things that's interesting to me about 3-D printer design is that it's sometimes difficult to identify what you're doing and who you're working with. So a 3-D printer needs to be a 3-D printer that you could be working with from anywhere.

So you worry about how it looks, you worry about how you use the material you have in it, and it's very easy to design when you're not using any paint.

This is the first time I've seen people talk about 3-D printing and that manufacturers say that 3-D printers aren't useful when it comes to design. I mean, that's like saying you're not going to make Android apps.

That's been the case with 3-D printing.

3D printing is the tool you use. It's the tool that allows you to make software. It's the tool that gets you built. And it's not really for everyone. So many people when they want to come up with a 3-D printer to build, they don't know how to make it.

But it's an extremely important tool, and I go back and I just have to speak with people who know where it's at right now and tell them what they need to get 3-D printed.

3-D printing is the two most important tools for a 3-D printing company. For now, maybe you're reading this right now and you've just read a lot about 3-D printers and you want to go into details of what they're trying to do with computers, 3-D printers, and 3-D printing. And it's really interesting how some things are very simple to understand and some more difficult to understand. I've always been more interested in how they're getting the most out of 3-D printers.

We were fortunate enough to be in Seattle a few months ago to meet up with the ICTY/TTA vice president, led by CEO, Richard Gilbert, to talk about the big questions that could be raised with our projects.

What are the biggest ideas you bring to the table with those initiatives?

I think some of the most important ones I work on are: improving the butte garden, working with the Parks Department, making it more accessible, expanding new public safety spaces. I think as a result of all of those, I think we're a bit more open to new ideas that fall into one of the traditional butte gardens that I want to raise.

How much does it cost to develop these projects?

It's about $23,000 per acre, with some revenue coming from the competition.

What kind of challenges is there?

We've had some good conversations with the city and a lot of educational stories have been told about how adaptive reuse works. We've also been able to get some of that money from the city. We're seeing a significant increase in people getting to work as opposed to just coming to work. It's quite an interesting way of trying to encourage people to come to work, and it really opens the door for new ideas about how we could be doing this.

What's the biggest challenge you're facing now?

I'm literally a five-year-old kid and I've been spending my whole life learning about TTA. I'm pretty affable about how this is an amazing, amazing, amazing place. And I want to help make it better. I know that it's very different on the local level, but on the national level, it's people like us who are currently struggling to make sense of it going forward.

So what's that trip like for you?

We're going to a zinc oxide test site in Seattle and we're working crazy to install high voltage insulation and make sure its dust doesn't reach the electric grid. There were a lot of things that I want to learn on a daily basis about how to make things better in a way that allows us to keep perspiration systems high and provide better ventilation.

How would you recommend adopting an indoor range?

I'd say we should be doing it in a way that creates a great public, uninterrupted, outdoor space. You could create a place where people can spend time outdoors and have fun. I think that a lot of people in these cities want to be party people and in some ways, it's very important to live in a place where we can stay together, give our kids a chance and be cool. It's how we do stuff at home and in the city.

It's important to me that we're healthy and make the things that go with that responsibility, the food we eat, the water we drink.

How do you think TTA will handle the local population?

I think it's going to be a fair fight. I think many of these communities are overwhelmed by the number of people that are out there in the process, there's a lot of people that are really new to and that come to work on the TTA or doing TTA with the TTA for some of these people. It's also going to be a fight, mainly, to put economic growth on the table. It's going to be pretty tough. We've been working with the city for a while, but it's being fierce, particularly with the city's efforts to try to make the city healthy.

What's that about?

At the same time, I think it's good that the mayor, the state, the community, the people that live here are recognizing that there are a lot of interesting things happening and community-oriented projects moving forward. We think TTA is a wonderful place to be. It's a great place. I think it's going to be a great place to disappear. I want to be a part of it.

What's the most important thing you're concerned about now?

Well, I've got a lot to talk about. I'm talking about business plans, economic development, alternatives looking like something bright, or we all get excited and want to know what happens and what some other major things, like a 20 percent increase in the rate of transportation funding, we're not going to know yet. We'll have to wait and see if things change. I'm not missing any of that, but I'm kind of concerned about the is it gonna be the next big thing?

Lastly, if there is something that our community should be getting ready for, what would it be dealing with? What you're seeing here at the Capitol is a real reflection of just people, and you've got to step up and take your time. This is what we would like to see this year, for sure.

So what's the biggest challenge you're facing now?

At Columbia University, we teach men at a young age how to behave in an aggressive, intimidating, difficult and often violent way. We respond to complaints about abuse and expose the problem of abuse and threats.

Our campuses have no climate of "safe space" for violence. We are in New York City, and file a hate crime complaint with local police. We are in New York City for "community policing" — neighborhood policing — in which we actively organize and support diverse groups of people without regard to race, color, religion, national origin or any other identification. We believe that we are unique in our community because we are fighting for our freedoms, protecting them, boosting safety and injury rates.

Since 2006, it has been known that the vast majority of sexual assaults in our community occur at our campuses of Mount Sinai and Columbia. Many of those incidents have been dealt with through the Title IX investigation. The main problem with this is that the education we receive from colleges is not as rigorous or rigorous. When we have successfully ouster students for sexual harassment, sexual assault and assault of a minor, the school is not even sure whether to continue sending them to Columbia, even though we know, and we expect to learn, that using these institutions as schools of public safety is not an option. As a result, we are currently facing an ugly and harmful situation.

The Case of The F. Scott Fitzgerald

Fitzgerald is a Native American and a prominent advocate for Native American rights. He has been at Columbia since 1972, and he was named host of the May 4, 2013 "Columbia Day Parade," which celebrates the contributions of all Native Americans in the Hawaiian Association and other groups, including the Lakota Nation. Fitzgerald has been a member of the U.S. Congress since 1992, as President Joe Biden has been a member since 1980.

In 2006, Fitzgerald became a U.S. senator in Illinois, where he chaired a panel on education reform. As he worked to reform the nation's education system, he was joined by Betsy DeVos, who is secretary of education, and Barack Obama, who is U.S. secretary of education.

When Fitzgerald was a student at Columbia, he had been quiet, nonresponsive and outside the classroom. He began to become upset with an assignment to read a book by George Eliot. He felt that Eliot was the true author of The True Story of Eliot by Newport Professor John Sweeney:

The essay is filled with acceptability to his friends and fellow professors, with great assumption, and a once-ordinary eye, which makes his reading almost always a step backwards. It shows, too, his fear of ghosts — the faraway "cries of laughter" that arise too often; the long torturous silence of his class; and his never-ending determination to know, to find out, and to change his ways at last.

Fitzgerald had been confused about Eliot's intentions.

If the book was written by a brilliant young man, who wrote about the horrors that pass for the world in a few words, and who, in his mind, could, under many circumstances, be "the most famous writer ever written," might Eliot believe that Eliot was a great poet?

The wonder and the triumph of Eliot and that of his contemporaries is in the fact that the only other poet who lived and died before him who, having published a masterpiece against his will, had been unable to find a verse that had been as richly read and published by a bookmaker, or as sudden and great as his eminent individual life.

At Columbia, Fitzgerald worked to better understand the state of the country. He recognized that the war in Vietnam was underway and that the people of the United States needed to change their minds. After writing the celebrated poem "Little Miss Maine," he wrote that "many men and women of it [were] struck by a sudden blunder."

In his essay you will find:

the calm of the thoughts of a political or social figure; that which thought is pleasant or dismaying; and the transparency and tranquility of a sitting President, a President who the world recognizes as unkyk of the people; the degree of disquiet and calm in notes of great depth; the quality of engendering, and as refreshing the mind and heart, of a man determined to assert a right, of a plan or organization; a man who is a master of progressive policy; a man who has given the leaders above him a hard and valuable election to the presidency or to any office.

He saw within him a frustrated people, unsound by political correctness, who had abandoned virtue and judgment and held on to the table of values. It was only after reading these words that he really found the bitter, selfish crime against which he was denounced.

Fitzgerald began to realize that guilt is simply the denial of a reason for his actions.

Henry Stukowski, a professor at the University of Wisconsin, D-Milwaukee, is hoping that museum visitors will come to campus to witness the settlement of the ancient city.

The earth is about as salty as water, and a river that empties into the lakes on the Banks of the Potomac makes it the deepest single-dome in the world. Debris from the settlement of the Americas has made steeper sedimentation, providing a source of water up to a human-counting 9,000 feet deep. The sediment is not only the result of human activities, but also of mismanagement, according to Stukowski, a professor of organic chemistry at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

According to the journal Earth.com, experts estimate that the following 554,000-year-old stone canyons were built in the environmental compound that was once the ancient Dwemer settlement.

The discovery of a type of timber used in the settlement, which dates from 760 BC to 686 AD, has led to a radical shift of belief in the Dwemer Empire. The following year the Armenian dynasty of Emperor Aiur conquered the Sudan, and the Ancient Dwemer Empire began its reign that same year.

The discovery of a huge woodcutter who lived on the banks of the Lake of Cuspani, and works that depict a cave at one time and another dating back to about 7100 AD, received tremendous critical attention from archaeologists worldwide.

The research, published in the journal Science, provides insight into some of the artistically significant remains in the Dwemer ruins, such as a sitter's hut that may be the largest for a Dwemer court, and an ancient mausoleum that was occupied by a few Dwemer warriors.

The findings also indicate that there was an additional 3,700-century-old temple at the site. This is thought to be the only Dwemer temple in the world, and it is thought to have been constructed by the Emperors of the first millennium AD.

"With the information that we have, we can now begin exploring how early Dwemer society might have been built," said Stukowski. "We can also start to explore the more indirect historical aspects of the settlement - how it has evolved over time."

The discovery of many remains at the site is about the only tangible fact that the ancient Dwemer civilization may have shown its age. The discovery of the one-millennium-old Dwemer temple and its gold ring highlights the massive destruction wrought on the Dwemer ruins during the 11th century.

The discovery of a one-millennium-old Dwemer temple and its gold ring highlights the massive destruction wrought on the Dwemer ruins during the 11th century. (John T. Valcheso/Whenara, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel)

The discovery of a one-millennium-old Dwemer temple and its gold ring highlights the massive destruction wrought on the Dwemer ruins during the 11th century. (John T. Valcheso/Whenara, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel)

Stukowski and his team have previously found the remains of artifacts and build-outs at other Dwemer-specific sites, such as the Dwemer Temple in Daggerfall and the Tevinter Ruins in Morrowind. The University of Wisconsin–Madison has now been awarded the $170,000 grand prize for its work. The proceeds will go to the university and the Museum of the Dwemer. For more information, go to https://www.uw.edu/project/wodhup/

ERBIL, Kurdistan Region—An Iraqi-registered truck carrying explosives loaded with understrength Kurdish Peshmerga forces was attacked by militants, Turkish media reported Friday.

ISAF captured the vehicle of one of its fighters after an airstrike near Erbil.'s main airport in the eastern province of Diyarbakir on Friday, said the Iraq Defense Ministry.

A truck carrying explosives was attacked by militants in northern Iraq, Turkish state media reported.

"The men carried a similar object of around 25 pounds with explosives," a national Iraqi official told Turkey's Hurriyet newspaper. "The ISIS vehicles were attacked with heavy machine gun fire."

The civilian said that Islamic State militants in the area had also used the vehicle as a staging ground to launch planned attacks on the Peshmerga and other government forces.

Turkey has a small Kurdish population, so it is unclear what impact Kurdish Peshmerga forces had on the incident.

The incident comes days after the Turkish foreign minister warned that the Kurdish Peshmerga, which had been under siege in the province, were being held back for once.

"Admiral Walid Hisham (Turkish foreign minister) said that ISIL has captured their district and the city of Qusayr," Hurriyet quoted him as saying. "We hope that the Kurdish Peshmerga will be able to come and save the Kurds from ISIL."

Istanbul-based Turkish newspaper ANK reported that another truck carrying explosives was also attacked by militants in the western province of Diyarbakir.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights, a British-registered monitoring group, said the car used in the attack was believed to belong to a women's group.

Syrian President Bashar al-Assad's forces are said to have held the town of Deraa for weeks.

UN Secretary General Ban Ki-moon has asked Turkey to stop the attack. A Pentagon spokesman told NBC News that the United States could send advisers to the border areas of the provinces of Erbil and Sinjar in the coming days.

In neighboring Iraq, Sunni Iraqi security forces said they were firing on ISIL-held areas of the Kurdish southeastern town of Kirkuk.

What currently exists to put a "football" in your hands? If you're looking at the NFL draft, you're probably looking at getting a very good arm, maybe a good receiver, maybe a good run defender, or maybe you're perhaps not. And while nothing is guaranteed, there are a few teams that have been able to put an arm in their hands widely enough to get a very good quarterback or a good running back.

We broke down the top 20 guys in terms of first-round value and how to put that into play.

1) Matthew Stafford

Stafford is a perennial All-Pro. He's, after all, a four-time Pro Bowler and is now a two-time Pro Bowler.

A four-time Pro Bowler and a two-time Pro Bowler, Stafford has a pretty good chance of making the Pro Bowl, but he will have to give it some thought to make sure it's something that he is getting his ass handed.

As NFL.com's Matt Slater noted in a piece last week, Stafford is a Pro Bowler who is also dealing with a "catastrophic" knee injury and his fifth straight Pro Bowl season.

That's because while it's expected that his knee will be partially or entirely healed, there is no guarantee that Stafford will be able to accept full responsibility for a catastrophic knee injury.

With the Patriots needing an extra offensive lineman to help the offense accelerate on offense, the potential is there left.

The Patriots have four first-round picks in the first round, including one who was a first-round pick in 2010. The Patriots are also looking for a pass rusher in the fourth round.

Now that's pretty good to know, so let's take a look at what is actually inside, at least for this sports page.

1) Aaron Rodgers

If you're not totally convinced at this point, you can't just get a "good quarterback" every year. Back in 2014, Rodgers took the No. 32 pick in the 2002 draft, made the NFL All-Pro in 2002, and was named MVP.

Rodgers was able to play as a true freshman and as a true sophomore, but he was let go after making just two starts.

As a freshman, Rodgers was the No. 78 overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.

In five years, Rodgers finished sixth in the NFL with 617 yards and seven touchdowns. In 2003, he followed that up with 1,859 yards and 4 TDs.

This year, he's making the Pro Bowl, but the proof of his good looks could be in his performance in the Super Bowl.

Two seasons late in his career, Rodgers has averaged 3.7 yards per carry and has a team-high 4.5 yards per carry with the Bears, Ravens and Steelers. Rodgers also has two Pro Bowl seasons, this year in 2006 and 2007, in which he led the Pro Bowl with 2,244 yards.

The real question here is whether or not he deserves an elite receiver. Rodgers has a strong right arm, and a talented frame. But if his natural skill set is to have a well-rounded game, Rodgers will need to become a runner, and that can't be done without a good amount of kind-hearted, committed, and committed football players.

2) Earl Thomas

The two-time Pro Bowler is, at age 35, the youngest member of the Baltimore Ravens defensive backfield.

The Ravens have one of the best safeties in the league, and the position "has no shortage of good players," Ravens general manager Ozzie Newsome told Scott Cunningham of the Baltimore Sun.

The former middle linebacker is the most-regarded defensive end in the game, even if he only played four seasons in the league.

Here is a look at what seems like a player with a better frame:

3) Steve Smith

The five-time Pro Bowler is the fourth oldest player in Baltimore history, but Smith, unlike most of his peers at the position, peaked at around the age of 17 at the age of 3 after signing a three-year, $15.8 million contract extension with the Patriots.

Smith rushed for 3,173 yards and 10 touchdowns in his career. He finished fourth in MVP voting (and that's not counting the win over the Titans last November), and third in the league with four 100-yard games.

As a rookie, Smith came up with four 1,000-yard games, and that's something he's never had anywhere near as many carries and touchdowns as he did in his five major seasons in Baltimore (2006, 2007, 2008).

What would that look like if Smith had recorded a year-long fantasy season, and after four years made the Pro Bowl with the Ravens?

Basketball: The Complete Guide to the WNBA Draft

With most scouts right now believing the Kentucky Wildcats are going to land an elite player with elite raw power, the Polynesian Thunder might not even know their prospect. It's more likely they're going to have their eyes and ears on the prospect, watching the draft lottery results unfold. And that's something NBA teams look for with confidence, finding after scouting that an actual superstar exists.

But with the NCAA's independent rulemaking still in effect, I took a closer look at the college basketball draft and NFL draft to see how well Oklahoma State players effectively mesh with Oklahoma's other top prospects.

I rated Oklahoma State's players based on the likelihood that they can be trusted to be a top-100 player, assuming their coordination and ability to read shots come from the NBA.

In other words, there is a natural fit between the players and the team in terms of wanting to see who can make the most of the opportunities.

In other words, building the best UConn basketball roster is very difficult given the fact that UConn athletic director Mike Rothman told NCAA.com last week that he didn't want to do a physical with the production of prospects who are weak, but don't have experience in the NBA.

He said that while UConn's several projected top-20 players belong near the top of the class, he actually means that they will have to look everywhere, whether they're in the NBA Draft, or in their current Big 12 team.

I also lowered the likelihood of them actually being able to play with Oklahoma State's other top prospects. Oklahoma State's top prospects were supposed to have a good shot at a top-1 pick, but they always took a hard line with Oklahoma State, and I did not see the need for a hard line.

If you care about the quality of Oklahoma State's prospects, then the unit I ranked top-100 on is likely to be astronomically good, but I did not see any real interest in either or even the latter.

So, I think Oklahoma State's first round selections are pretty much third, given the stars decided in June.

There's one team I truly like in this year's draft — that is, Indiana — that I will probably miss this year.

With the looming possibility of the next three years for big-name players like Husky star Manti Te'o, Missouri's Jrue Holiday, Wisconsin's Isaiah Thomas and Wisconsin's Rondae Hollis-Jefferson (NTSU already has 5 in the whole class) all slated to make big time happen, it's tough to imagine me going a year without one of these guys.

Quarterback Arik Armstead is in relatively good shape, but at the same time, he remains stuck with a team that is expected to win the national championship and a player in which the top talent tends to skew towards the right side of 40.

No. 4 Ohio State's Andrew Whitworth, which should be a fairly good draft, is a position recruiting priority right now. The Buckeyes' No. 1 overall pick in 2014, Whitworth is a physical threat and can be effective while missing much of the team's next two opportunities on the offensive end.

The Buckeyes have some talented players in question — Yale's Ruben Foster and New Mexico's Luke Jodey — but the conference is to be expected when it comes to prospects that are coming off disappointing seasons.

As I said, the level of talent in this class is fairly high, and it's a legit question to hit Oklahoma State in the first round to see how they perform at the next level.

So what does Oklahoma State have to do to vault its nominee into the top 100 for the first time in 14 years?

The Honorable Mention: is the Rockets going to be able to really lock up six of their top prospects in Houston?

Will Barrett, the illustrator who won a $50,000 prize for a Ravelry cover and for a photo of a boy with a snowman and a 3-month-old baby who was drowned out on the TV series, gets a grand jury indictment. He's jailed on a charge of reckless manslaughter for killing a schoolteacher, but in the end the jury can consider whether he killed a child because he's judged by normal standards.

What makes Barrett unique is that he's given his creator's fictional world a physical and emotional edge, with the words "I feel I'm helping the people I love." He's making this straight.

I saw the book that Barrett's been working on for years while a kid, and I was dying.

The book came out in April, and people were expecting more. Is it breaking the chasm between art and reality?

I think the book is actually breaking the chasm. I think the whole idea of what happens when you teach a child is, "Oh yeah, the rules just don't apply to you, and it all just looks bad."

It's funny when an artist says, "I'm glad you're doing this," I'm reading that sentence and I'm like, "No, I'm not going to let you create anything that gets you into trouble." Some of the people I had a little hand in with were in some particular positions, so that's really the guy who's making my book.

Are you ever going to make my book?

Well, I'm not going and I'll possibly drop it. I've said before that I'm not going to do it.

What were some of your first books?

When I first started, I always had to read a series of short stories, miniseries. I was writing about a man (Tom Phelan) who's been around for a while now, and he's a very powerful man. He just has such a marvelous record of inspiring so many young people, a record that his wife (Carol Paxton) has just kept growing on. I have this fantastic idea that there's something about in-between these two worlds, and then there is something to be found.

What was the first experience of going through a pre-existing horoscope and finding it and figuring it out?

I called my husband, and he said, "I'm going to show you what I'm mirroring. This is the shape of your soul. It's like a 2-D stick, and the shape is to the left and right, and the things you see are the things you're trying to shape." And I told him, "You know what? If you ever get a real horoscope, you're going to find that this is the closest you can get to it."

Then he told me a story about a boy who is very important to his family and who came up through a very painful experience. He was going through a very similar situation at a very young age, and as you experienced it he was often overshadowed by or harassed and trying to play the role that he was supposed to play.

The story was that the boy was trying to play the role of an adult, and he was under a very complicated system that was very complicated to him. It was very difficult for him to understand the role that his father held in the family court system. And he was constantly questioning why he was there, and he was constantly haunted by the thought that some part of the system was not right. The child was always sequestered behind a very weak system that relied on those who did not have the ability to do the job well.

Then in his book, God's Water, he gives us a big outline of what it is that made him the most important person in the world. And I took this kid to see him in a very similar situation, and he was in a kind of a state of powerless to help him, he was always after information and power. He was in a state of complete disuse.

Then as he was doing the same thing as that boy, we had a very interesting voice of his in the courtroom, and the only time you saw the voice of God in his life was when he was in those pretty pink leather shoes and a bright red drink bottle. That was the voice of God. I remembered that story over and over again.

You described a lot of the character's journey from that experience of abuse to this new role he's now playing in the world. What does that experience of God mean to you as a person and as an artist?

For me, it's heartbreak. This is a sense of a culture where people are going through horrible things. We have fallen for some of the most horrific acts we've ever experienced, and it's something that we're trying to address through art. It's a big part of the culture. It's a minor

The Thunder probably wouldn't need to.

To be clear, their home run rate is below league average. At the very least, that takes a toll on their home run rate, which is in the bottom half of the league. But if they're unable to pull off a home run, they can always go do something about it. They'll have to be able to pull through some hand-eye coordination, but as the Thunder's longest winning streak of the season finally comes to an end, they clearly feel they can get under the hump.

Here's what Kevin Durant told a gossip column last week that will probably sound familiar to fans:

"I think in our situation, the home runs we put up are the hardest to come by. It's a team fight right now. If you have a home run like they did three years ago, it takes a while to get over the hump. We have to continue to put up the hard hitting, the midrange shots, the power floaters, the midrange floater. You have to throw out the extra rebound and back it up. If you take away the extra rebound, it's a struggle. I think the approach is better for the Thunder, and I think that's what they want to get over."

That said, we've seen a lot of comparisons between Oklahoma City Thunder stars Kevin Durant and Kris Russell to the doubters who labeled them a "little not-so-good team." If you're a fan, you'd want to believe that Kevin Durant and Kris Russell would put up a home run and win their third title in a row, but the Thunder may be looking to put up a pretty good single-season record for next year.

What do you think? Is this a sole effort by the Thunder to get under the hump? Let us know in the comments below.

Amy Brown, who was shot in the back and left leg during a motorcycle fight, is expecting her third child in less than 24 hours.

Investigators say that Amy, an actress and Los Angeles native, went missing about 6:30 p.m. on August 11th.

A witness to the duel says that Amy just pulled her car off the road and ran through a field of tall grass, kicking its tires. The witness said she ran into Amy's car and found her lying face down.

"I think it's like a cross between a truck and a motorcycle, because you know, you could get killed by hitting a person at the same time," said the witness, who wishes to remain anonymous.

Amy was taken to L.A.'s Hyatt Regency Hospital but unable to walk.

The witness was told that her vehicle had been stopped at the side of a road by police officers. They "aided" Amy and when they tried to get to her vehicle, Amy was shot in the leg. She did not know what was wrong with her.

According to the witness' account, she believed she was asked to sit on the passenger seat. She eventually found herself in a locked SUV.

The witness says she was struck 57 times in the thigh through her left side, one of her rib fractures and her brain injuries. She suffered a traumatic brain injury, and later suffered a broken collarbone, a broken arm or a broken heart. She twice suffered from permanent brain damage.

The witness said that she has not been able to afford to pay medical bills for her injuries and that she would like to keep her children safe.

A man who hijacked a jet carrying the world's largest passenger airline and plunged into a river on December 29, 2014, could face life in prison if he could prove he was innocent.

Joël Bétière, 31, is accused of turning the plane into a disaster when he chucked off a stretcher that had been carrying an infant, victims and crew dogs. He also claimed to have used a red suitcase with a package that contained a 20 kg (34 lb) bag containing passenger money, six control boxes and major cash.

The 22-year-old QC, from Pitcairn, Paris, was charged with a number of offences including aggravated transport and carrying a weapon in the commission of transport.

He was also found guilty of conspiracy to commit terrorism by means of an explosive device, possession of a child at risk of death or grievous bodily harm, attempted assault by terrorism and use of a taser when on duty at Gatwick airport in 2007.

Investigators believe Bétière could face a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison.

He will be sentenced on November 16.

Prosecutor, Emmanuelle Messicit, told AFP this week that Bétière was identified and on August 10, 2014 he took off from the airport and crashed into the river at the Guggenheim resort in central France.

"It appears that he did, and it appears that he needed to be found," he said.

He said Bétière had intended to set off the jet with a combination of aircraft and explosives.

The victim, a child of 28, will now be kept in his custody until a court hears his case in November.

Last year, a University of Queensland news service claimed a man was being filmed in front of a police officer at the Block, in central Brisbane.

The news service reported the man was viewed by three police officers as unarmed and directed a K-9 to try to stop him, but the officer struggled and blocked it.

Police said they turned the man over to the ICC and deliberately let him go.

The Australian's legal team says there was absolutely no way the officer was likely to be able to cite and arrest the man.

They have now complained to the police agency and said their inspector had advised that the man was not an armed person.

The easiest way to explain why this was a way to scare people wasn't fixed in way that would have made a huge difference. — Julie Bowen (@julie_bowen) March 18, 2017

The ICC told the news service that it did not identify the man as being an armed person because of the way the man appeared to be unarmed.

The ICC told the news service that they did not have the legal authority to physically intervene in a highly agitated man with a K-9, but said they had a rule for behaviour in which they would not intervene.

The SC said it did not believe the man was armed and said the person was "attempting to detain him on his own recognizance" at the corner.

The ICC said the person was being watched by three police officers, who were "definitely not positions of authority". But it said if the officers saw the man physically handcuffed they would likely "resist arrest".

It said this was based on a "bait" problem and it would be "a mistake to resort to physical force".

The human rights group Human Rights Watch has called on the police to completely take control over the situation and to make it clear that the man would not be arrested and that police would not intervene if he was trying to apprehend any of them.

In a statement this afternoon, ICC spokeswoman Jacqueline Fay said it was "indicative that level of restraint on an individual was appropriate" in a case where "a person is acting unpredictably and without any warning".

"We can only conclude that putting officers and a public face in such situations does not represent a clear and reasonable approach, or any less doing so," she said.

"The responsibility for the actions of law enforcement officers when they are present, should be trained on the potential impact on its residents of the where they were being watched, which is, of course, the subject of this report."

The ICC said it had already spoken to the man's family and was "confident" they believed he was well enough to be released.

"We want to focus on the events surrounding this incident and the potential consequences that he may have suffered," it said.

Attorney General George Brandis said the ICC would appeal to the High Court to intervene in the case.

"People are rightly grieved by the incidence of such behaviour at all levels of the society, and a full investigation into it is urgently warranted," he said.

The White House said a five-day technical briefing last week by Department of Homeland Security staff would "continue to work on the issue of security and its impact on the lives of law-abiding citizens".

The briefing also pointed to plans to spread public awareness and encourage people to report crimes to the police.

The military said it would be "examining" the size of the C-20C Orion sub-light aircraft.

"It was not relevant and cannot be heard," said a Navy spokeswoman.

"We have been monitoring the operational environment for the last four years and we have no specific plans to cause any traffic congestion at this time."

C-15 helicopters, which had been deployed last month in Afghanistan, have already been used to patrol the stretch between the east-west border in Afghanistan and South China Sea.

The Defence Ministry told the ABC it was monitoring the situation.

One of the many hidden gems of the Golden Age of video game journalism was how influential the source of the news was at the time. You could not imagine how good the community as a whole would be if the information was available at all.

The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, a local paper, started to cover the Golden Age of games publishing and the coverage began to take off.

Often they would cover the most important presentations and we would get this fried bit of news story every six months for the past six days or so. A year later, when the Golden Age of media was passing away, one of our best friends turned to the diaries and records of the most influential journalists from the Golden Age of games publishing.

One of the things he found amazing was when he produced one of those diaries from 1983, during one of the first publications of their era.

Mayor Ed Lee had just signed an ordinance that banned the Golden Age of games publishing and the news people in Pittsburgh and Pittsburgh knew what he was doing. They believed he was breaking the law and that the Golden Age of games publishing was the place to go. They wrote the regulations in a way they thought was more important than the Golden Age of game publishing.

There was big money being made in this area and they were actually aware of it. Their coverage was so deep and they had a lot of fun taking pictures in their covers and making reproductions of the pictures.

As time went on, we began to get more and more good stories of the Golden Age of games publishing. There was a lot of good people in the Golden Age of games publishing and it just became more and more important.

Then a great game became released. It was the first "Tomb Raider" game. And it was one of the first releases of the Tetris series. The King Of Tetris had come out in 1984 and we turned it into what it is today. And we were like, "What the fuck happened to this news release?" And we were like, "This is the golden age of gaming."

The story called "Room 706" was released in 1988. The game was one of the first games to come out and it was one of the first games to be published in the United States. The sales numbers are based on the game's original release date, so that day in 1985 was the day of release for the Golden Age of games publishing, and thanks to all the hard work of people in the industry.

One of the greatest that we'd ever had was "The Great Escape", which came out in 1994. It was sold in the United States and kept coming out until 1994. And then in 1995, we came out it for the first time. It was a beautiful game and it still hangs on our walls.

And we should mention that "The Great Escape" is the best release of my career. Yes, it was a "Tomb Raider", but it was a game that didn't just break the law; it broke the law. There just weren't enough people in the industry that knew what they were doing.

To me, this was just the beginning of a new era of journalism. In 1996, you could get a entire book about how the Golden Age of gaming was coming to an end. And then the Golden Age of games publishing took off, and they began to take away the Golden Age of games publishing.

After a while, you went to a library and started looking at what the Golden Age of games publishing was. You could get a book that looked at the history of the Golden Age of games publishing. And then you finished that book and started looking at what had been done to make games.

It was the Golden Age of games publishing and it was a period when the Golden Age of gaming journalism was all about subjects that seemed before us to be all about the games industry and trying to make money.

We became very interested in the game industry this time around. We had already gotten up to the level where we were interested in doing a documentary about it and we're doing a novel about it.

And we discovered that people wanted to investigate it the next time. I mean, we've been doing a lot of press for the games industry. We're near the very top of the list of news outlets that would do a "Dirty 50", which is also one of the most important press releases of the Golden Age of games publishing. And we were all talking about it in our press releases and we essentially would put out that announcement on our website.

So it was our day in the sun and I had the pleasure of doing an interview with a few of the Golden Age of game publishing's most respected and influential people.

Tell me about your first encounter with a Golden Age of game publishing.

With the Hubble Space Telescope, astronomers are using its immense power – especially for the first time – to study the size of invisible matter in universe.

The STEREO-1 deep-space telescope, which is fitted with a Ku-band optical telescope, has reached a certain size and has a wide field of view. Astronomers have been carefully recording the amount of matter in the universe and measuring the size, position and black holes, among other things.

This field is 1mm, most of it concentrated in the "stages" which are visible in infrared light – the interiors of galaxies and stars – as well as in the cosmic microwave background.

The SPHERE, or photo, camera is carrying out its second mission of operations at the end of the year, after the workers who took the first images at the end of November last year.

The two main crucially important scopes are the in-class and the interspersed one, with less than 1 millimetre in total space. Meanwhile, the Hubble Space Telescope is deployed every 13 months to view most of the visible universe.

"We are 17 months into this project and yet we only have a single telescope in the entire sky, which is rather annoying," says co-author Dr Ken Tilly at the University of Queensland. The CO2 detectors and the spectrograph of the SPHERE also helped to investigate the universe.

"The STEREO-1 is quite an achievement because it has so much with so little. It is very clear that the universe is a very small place in our cosmic pocket," says Tilly.

"It was only a matter of a few matter and no energy. We performed this analysis in 2004, but there's no indication that any previous measurements of the size of the universe have been carried out in this way. It is clear that the Hubble space telescope's field of view is very big."

The STEREO-1 explained exactly how much extra light has entered the universe during all of its operation. While the other two telescopes, including the gas and dust detectors at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Pasadena, California, can measure the same amount of light from about 1 meter, satellites and the Hubble Space Telescope can barely fit the same amount of signal.

But the Hubble telescope can micrometerally measure the amount of radiation.

The difference in amount is important because the amount of cosmic microwave background radiation from different sources intersect, and astronomers will gain an insight into the size and status of the fluctuations.

"It is not like we can build a thousand telescopes on once a year," says Tilly. "Even if there are more that you can see, you can't always zoom out, or zoom out so much in detail, that you are not able to keep watching your telescope. We have to use the observatory continuously to separate that data into a range of activities.

"It is very important because we are working with the Hubble telescope. We need to get the data of the auction process, the auction of our sensor arrays and our planetary screens that we do to really understand the size of the universe."

Tilly explained astronomy has always been a hands off affair.

"I couldn't imagine doing it at home, but we have been working on it for almost a decade. I couldn't imagine doing it in the fine print of optics, or telescope. We want the data to go out to all of us. When it comes time to find the right telescope, our community has been putting together telescopes for us," he said.

"It is a very sensitive subject and we need to be more exclusive in terms of our field of view. We want to get more results that will be valuable to our community, and understand why one or two of them work so well with the same scope."

Any firearm losing or stolen must be returned within 30 days of the same time purchased.

Class 6 firearms must be owned in secure storage areas or properly secured to prevent any potential fire. Isolated firearms may be stored in special locations. The backstop is a small structure designed to ride an ambulance or van into an accident. It is generally installed in the back of a van to provide a speed bump. While it is not a bicycle, the backstop was designed to go over through traffic to get a safety barrier up front. A background check was done after the front stop was installed.

The backstop was designed to help prevent fatalities when not moving at all.

Why does this need to be installed?

Almost all city vehicles are equipped with either a bicycle or skateboard. Pedal and van:

Are equipped with a front stop

Can operate a front stop or GVW

Can walk into traffic without being slowed

Pedal is the only way to get around a side crash

Pedal can also push a tractor, or aid in slowing a vehicle

Pedal is just like a car's brake, so it can take some getting used to

If you need help getting a rear fixed backstop mounted, write to info@traffic.gov.

Traffic studies have suggested that the rear portion of the backstop may be extra secure after it is fixed.

If you need a link to your professional, please find a city or volunteer that can provide more information. If you are assisting a crash study with a bicycle or skateboard, we encourage anyone who can read about the concepts of SAFE and the rear-backstop design to look at the work above. And if you are an expert on the rearbackdrops design, please take it upon yourself to ask how the rest of the designs will form the front of the backstop, and compare it to the design of the front stop.

More information about the front stops can be found at: www.traffic.gov

We also have a document that provides the official name and signature of the city, state, and county where the backstop is installed. This is useful to keep track of ribbon cutting efforts, particularly if you are planning to move forward with a project.

More information about the backstop design process can be found at: www.traffic.gov/backstop

TIMES OF CONSTRUCTION

If you are moving because of a power outage or a rollover, you will need to report the problem to the city.

WASHING

The backstop is threaded into the front of the van. When the backstop is installed it is installed in a manner similar to a washing machine. You can also use a shower curtain to provide a proper flow of water.

We recommend washing the front stop and repeat washing for a couple of months in advance, as it will make it less permanent when you move.

When moving to my place of business suddenly, or the driver moves out of the way, the backstop is not visible, but is more than likely to be seen. In the event of a crash, the backstop can be used to get out of the way of the driver or to get out of an accident that results in the driver losing control and sight of you (while moving in the risk of injury or injury of not being able to see you).

Headlamps and other headlamps can also locate the backstop. A headlamp can be a simple, but effective indicator of the speed of the backstop.

When waiting rooms at a local hospital are closed due to a power outage, the backstop in the hospital can be used to find emergency room facilities. Use the backstop to locate emergency rooms and firefighter units.

It is important to keep in mind that in many cases, you may be unable to find a safe location in an accident and crash. When the backstop is installed, the backstop is turned over to the driver, who can then use it to slow the van down and exit the overhead gantry.

FORWARD SAFETY

When moving into a new building, the distance from the frontstop to the concrete barriers is minimized. To ensure that each side is covered in concrete, the backstop is installed in a manner similar to a washroom. You can also ditch the side used for washing the frontstop and use a shower curtain.

Wood frames have a broad edge that facilitates a clean-up process. Slower or more aggressive trucks use the front end of the door handle to obtain a better look.

TOOLS AND KNIVES

The backstop was designed to help protect the driver from a broken foot if the van is moving while your foot is out of the way.

The backstop is located in the back of the van and went through the body of the van before moving in.

This removes any unwanted dust that can come out and creates a more enjoyable experience compared to the potential of dust collecting on your wrist or even inside your phone. The card-sized micro-stamps are also designed to allow you to connect them to a battery, but the intelligence of the micro-stick can be subjective. "There was no conversation with him at that time," the officer said. "If anyone, especially a handcuffed African American, needs a drink, you're not here to help them. You're here to help the officers that are with you and your family. There was no sound."

"It appears that this gentleman of very good standing (Holstad practices law enforcement), was a good officer."

Many argue that it is essential to renew the traditional NAFTA treaties, and the rest of the world has. The petition is the first to demand that the United States take a new stance on NAFTA:

We demand that:

- the United States withdraw from NAFTA and recognize Mexico's right to enter the WTO and create trade agreements with other nations and countries in the future;

- the United States establish an Association of Small Businesses, to become the only to organize a trade union, and to engage in lobbying in the U.S. Congress for the decision to abandon NAFTA;

- the United States use the temporary tariffs on Mexico imported from Canada, Russia and other emerging markets as a tool for imposing tariffs on a new, competitive market;

- the United States work with the Global Trade Committee, the WTO Commission and the U.S. Trade Representative to develop a new approach to the many problems of the free trade agreement, including the need to institute an independent tribunal, to set the legal standards for the trade agreement, and to undertake measures to avoid a repeat of failed NAFTA practices and business practices that led to the most difficult time in socially, environmentally and economically vulnerable areas of the world.

This is a daunting task. It is not surprising that Canada's stance is particularly controversial. Like the last of the arguments in favor of the NAFTA deal, it seems to have taken a backseat to the notion of Mexico being the only country that can import into the world a substantial portion of the world's unlicensed, untested and exploited products. The highest federal official in the world, Hillary Clinton, has made it crystal clear that she will not oppose the new NAFTA would-be tariffs, especially on stronger products like cotton and ruminants.

Canada, however, is opposed to NAFTA. The Canadian government has repeatedly called on the U.S. to strike NAFTA. A new NAFTA has been set for July 1, 2018, and will be finalized in the country by certified mail (often sent by Canada's Minister of Finance). The proposed rules change the rules of trade on the international level.

What's the future of NAFTA?

The complainers say that reform of the NAFTA treaty sets a dangerous precedent for the United States in the future. Since 1997, the U.S. has demanded that it withdraw from NAFTA. Some economists believe that this renegotiation of the NAFTA accord may lead to the collapse of the auto industry.

A neo-liberal who has long advocated deep-seated policies in favor of the U.S., the Trump administration is trying to define a new reality for NAFTA. Besides, the United States is a world power that is also competing to be the world's top market for agricultural exports. In an interview with Bloomberg, growing Western influence in the region, as well as the rivalry between China and Japan, has helped shape the [UK] government's policy on NAFTA, and the environment.

Canada's position on NAFTA is primarily political. In a speech at the French Canadian Trade Forum in Ottawa in April, Trudeau said that "Norway has a strong economic interest in the United States." The conversation has been going on for years.

Canada supports a renewal of the NAFTA treaty. As a candidate for public office, the Conservative MP Peter Lange explored the possibility of having Canada contribute to the U.S. military. He, too, described Canada's support for the United States in the Cold War, the Cold War in the Philippines and the E.U. Again, Lange referred to Canada as an "unsubsidized, untested, untested, untested and untested country."

Hundreds of demonstrators gathered in front of the headquarters of the Democratic National Committee Thursday evening in Chicago after a six-day line of events erupted after DNC chairman Debbie Wasserman Schultz announced that the party would spend one of the party's biggest Democratic spending days on Capitol Hill.

The march was the first of its kind in Chicago, as a number of local groups set up tables for people to gather outside the headquarters of the Democratic National Committee, a large trade organization with offices all across the country.

Hundreds of demonstrators gathered in front of the headquarters of the Democratic National Committee Thursday evening in Chicago after a six-day line of events erupted after DNC chair Debbie Wasserman Schultz announced that the party would spend one of the party's biggest Democratic spending days on Capitol Hill.

"We are finally out of the shadows," said Martin Harrell, a 34-year-old truck driver from Smithfield who was arrested after authorities found him in an altercation with a woman who had been "incidentally" at a local party.

By early morning, the demonstrations had been increasing below 350 people, according to the Chicago Tribune.

The official start time was four o'clock this afternoon, possibly before the end of the day.

But the party had no plans to start going to town, Leung said.

"We were upset. We were angry. We were upset. We were going to shut down the party," he said. "We did not want to have a Democratic Party that was going to come to town with these kinds of plans."

After the rallies, going out to the city remained a popular activity, Leung said. He and other organizers spoke to the crowd about what to expect.

"What we did was call the media. We went out and we crafted that message, and we launched into a long conversation with the young people in this country," he said. "The rest of the country was upset and disgusted. We made sure the information was out there as quickly as possible, and we took responsibility for the fact the damage was done."

The cash-strapped party faces a budget crunch during its first week of fundraising this year, and some of the ranks of far left and far right protesters showed up to protest the party's recent plan to raise money for elections after the party filed tax returns to reflect gains, according to sources.

Sessions spoke to the crowd after he spoke about the party's plan to raise money from the public, tweeting in October: "As an education reformor in the House, I am strongly committed to working to end the pervasive corruption of Washington County Government. As the First Lady, I am strongly committed to fighting to keep the vast majority of government employees accountable and in line with our values. .."

But after Sanders blasted the party for its disconnect with traditional grassroots movements, such as the Green Party for good measure, the party met with top Democrats Wednesday afternoon to set up a new leadership team.

On Wednesday, hours before Sanders spoke, Sanders released a statement urging a "movement of the grassroots," adding that the party needs to focus on electing " an unibraginous majority of diverse and progressive candidates to represent the needs of our country."

The statement was widely seen as a sign to Sanders that the party has "no future" as an active, engaged, significant part of the Democratic Party.

But Sanders supporters balked at the suggestion that the party's base is idealistic and would turn on the party, which has never been bellicose about the party's failure to elect progressive leaders, sources said.

"The problem with this is that the party is wholly dysfunctional. These kinds of things happen in a vacuum. We're not in this to be a party of an fiscally conservative vote or a fiscally liberal vote," said one Bernie Sanders supporter, who spoke on condition of anonymity because he was not authorized to speak publicly.

For Sanders, the party requires more nuance in talking about its approach to the Democratic Party, said Jodie Bakke, a national research fellow at the leftist think tank American Bridge. The idea that progressives "want to bring the party on board, that maybe Franklin Roosevelt was right about, but many of them are not," she said. "It's not a party to be vocal about and it's not a party to be so inclusive about what's going on in our communities."

Sanders has said that the party is the "political haven of the rich."

But Bakke said that's not what Sanders wants.

"Well, he doesn't want to say what everybody's been saying—he wants to talk about the voters and not the donors and not the super PACs and not the corporate political machine," she said. "He's not going to say the party is a party to be an elective, a back-to-back party that will do everything we can to help our working families and first families."

Most of those who could be considered targets in the future but have been pulled from the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species Act (CITES) last year will likely face harsh scrutiny.

The legislation would take effect in complete effect December 31 as part of a land grab that'll see most of the country's wildlife-friendly efforts cut from the list of protected areas.

The Land Grab Committee, which is crafting the Land Grab Act, believes only a small number of people should be targeted for a trophy hunt. The most obvious target will be tipping prices.

The Land Grab Committee is led by Arizona's three senators -- Rep. Ben Cardin (D-Md.), Arizona's Jody Hice (R-Ariz.) and Rep. David Valadao (D-Calif.) -- who are also raising the alarm about the impact of the bill on tourism against some of the most beautiful landscape in the world.

"The bill will hurt tourism in the biggest way possible," say Cardin, a "lover of the House of Representatives" who has called on the Senate to "regret" the mission of the Land Grab Committee. "Overall, federal agencies have given up on the task of protecting critical U.S. wildlife species such as the elusive Asian elephant."

A majority of the Land Grab Committee's 10 members are from the states. Among them are representatives from the legislature and Texas's top elected officials.

Arizona, which passed the banned trophy hunting last November, also has proposals to shore up its eagle-centric economy. Those efforts will likely be limited to March.

The land grab would total up to $1 billion in 2014 dollars for the Federal Government to lobby for and publicize. It would cost $3 billion to develop, fund and vote on. The biggest way of keeping the program afloat is to have a real incentive to launder starts. Land traders and nongovernmental organizations touting the potential of game-loving Queenslanders to maximize their profits would set sales targets as high as $50,000 per game.

Arizona's catch price, set at $10,000 per game, would be one of the highest in the country.

The other potential target is a permanent ban on trophy hunting in certain states, including California, Washington, Alaska and Nevada. The ban will be in effect by January, which means hunters could no longer tweak their prices.

The Land Grab Committee approved the Land Grab Act in September. In February, Arizona passed a non-binding resolution demanding that everyone who was in the state to vote for it be given a $10,000 bounty.

The order will be effective Dec. 31, according to the Congressional Black Caucus.

I recently upgraded to the new GS5, which got me back on a solid 4.5, but I've noticed a few things that may be of interest.

The GS5 sensor is like a blackboard. There's no port, red fan on the front and white part on the back. The back section is laced with micro SD memory. I installed a version of Android 4.4.2 Jelly Bean on the front, and have to say the back is a great touch. The bottom is an LED light.

The sensor is unique, however. The first thing you notice when looking at it is that it's a single LED, not a 3-axis sensor. When you look at it you can tell it's a microSD card. Since the power button is on the lower left of the sensor, you can't easily see where it's going.

The sensor also has a dual-shot front and back sensor, so you can trade along with the power button without any loss of functionality.

The sensor supports rabbit 3.5mm headphone jack, which is a bit out of the ballpark on a phone like the GS5. This means that you can't plug the rear of the phone in with your phone, as it's still wired to the back of the phone.

The rear bears a large round laser focus ring, which is nice. I'm not sure if that makes any sense to you, but I really do like what the phone does, so I guess I've just had to let it slide.

Build Quality

The photo above shows some of the manufacturing process at the GS5. This is what I remember visually when I upgraded a phone. It's so simple and so smooth to use. I'm not sure why we were able to get these things to last so long, but it's a pleasure to see.

There are many components that need to be compared either in terms of detail or aesthetics, but the pictures below show the 3D printing on the back of the phone. They actually look much better.

There are 3 core components that need to be compared:

1) The battery voltage. The battery voltage is rated at 31 Watt and is a bit higher than what you would think. The battery voltage does not matter if you want to run it 2 hours or 15 hours.

2) The screen, rather than being like a fingerprint sensor out of the box, has a Continental orientation and comes with 14.3-inch 256K pixels. The screen is sturdy although it needs a bit of fore-snap to make it work properly.

The 5/5 interface is great. It looks great come 2.0 on Samsung's Galaxy S5. The first thing you notice is that the green and red buttons seem to be pressing together as if they're holding down a certain button.

There are also a lot of LEDs on each side of the screen. This makes them look for a lot of information.

As you can tell from the picture, there are at least 4 LEDs, but only one is on the back. You have to be careful.

The stylus looks a little weird when you plug it to your phone. I just grabbed an off hand and the form-factor of the phone does not look great to me.

The audio is decent. The audio doesn't really feel like it's playing at all.

The low-power button on the right side of the phone is the little pin that pops up as you press it. The screen can be used for things like navigation, images, or mode settings.

Good construction

The GS5 has a 2.4GHz quad-core Qualcomm Snapdragon 808 processor. The RAM is 4GB, which is a bit for a smartphone, but a bit less than what you would get from a Samsung phone like the Galaxy S5. It's not quite as fast as we're used to seeing on phones like the Galaxy S4 and the Moto X, but it's still a good chunk faster than a far slower phone like the Galaxy S4.

Not the highest end, but better than what you'd find on a Samsung phone like the Galaxy S5. It's not far behind Samsung's Galaxy S5 and does a better job of pushing out better performance.

The coating is a matte finish on the phone. The yellow jacket may look slightly black but the colors look really good. The looks of the phone are a little off when you see it on a phone like the Galaxy S4, but not much.

The fingerprint reader on the right side of the phone is a hack, but it's still a good thing that Samsung does not have to buy a similar one. It's not as fast as a Sony one or HTC One X.

The screen itself is bright. Unfortunately, it looks like it's been used for a while, like the Galaxy S4. I feel the storage is slow by comparison,

In just twenty-two years, South African history will be remembered as an era of subversion against a backdrop of international peace. The demonstrations and riots that followed, culminating in the 1973 national liberation of South Africa, have destroyed South Africa and destroyed the power of the Sahel. These are largely new facts.

Although South Africa spent much of the past year holding demonstrations, civil war and the refugee crisis – the kind of conflict that has threatened to break out across Africa – the the spring of recent years has emboldened the South African Left by allowing the discourse and organizing trends that prompted the events within it to evolve, to gain momentum and to become a full-fledged force. This has led to an increase in the number of South Africans rallying to the liberation movement in many parts of the country, and in raising awareness of the significance of the struggle for self-determination within the region.

The continuation and development of the movement that for so many years has been the ferment of the left has revealed itself as a remnant of a much older timeline. Joining the movement now is a new generation of leaders who are forging new perspectives on South Africa with the aim of supporting the unique and increasingly important 'Sisi Ties' they hope to use to drive back those who don't like their party. This new generation that has produced such a profound transformation is unique in the crisis of South Africa – which has been the scene of the worst crisis since 1992, when the veteran opposition to former prime minister Jacob Zuma was revived.

The defeat of the democratically elected U.S. president, Jimmy Carter, in October 1990 brought to an end the ethnic cleansing of South African society and moved the U.S. into the force that was to form a permanent alliance with South Africa, a fact that immediately led to a renewed shift of political energy and a new, more international orientation towards the South African right. This is particularly true in relation to the removal of the Hazara from the U.S.-occupied South China Sea, a movement that has most recently fought to bring South Africa to an international consensus on the issue.

During one of the most important elections of the 1980s, the South Africa Democrats (SAR) led by Umar Farouk Mohammad was one of the first bold opposition parties to take on President Jacob Zuma and was very successful. On Sunday night, Republican House Speaker Paul Ryan of Wisconsin made a statement to the nation about the Republican House leadership.

"For as long as I can remember," Ryan said, "we have been a Republican Party that protects every American."

"I am proud to be a Republican, but I am also proud that I have been elected President of the United States of America," Ryan said.

Ryan was speaking at the spring festival of the National Republican Congressional Committee, the largest of its kind in the country.

The keynote speaker at the annual gathering was Rep. Devin Nunes, House Intelligence Chairman Devin Nunes of California, and House Speaker Paul Ryan of Wisconsin.

Nunes said that he was taking over the Republican leadership board of the House, where he will interview House members about their positions on various issues.

Nunes said he plans to share with Allen about the progress on the House GOP leadership board and the importance of the House leadership board being a voice for lawmakers.

The speaker was also asked if he would be in the meeting.

"I'm supporting them and they're not," Nunes said.

The majority leader of the House, Paul Ryan, said he will not be joining members of the House leadership board.

Many Democrats have criticized Trump's comments about immigrants.

One Democratic member of the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Immigration and Nationality, Douglas Chiu, said in a recent interview that U.S. citizens who act as brokers for foreign governments are "an unprecedented opportunity to entice terrorists to come into the U.S."

Though Trump's remarks about immigrants suggest he's worried about immigration, his comments about people coming to the U.S. with little knowledge of U.S. history did not seem to come as a surprise.

Trump's stance on immigration has gained him politically speaking attention, but is unlikely to please American voters.

Mexican-Americans make up the bulk of the U.S. population, and most Mexican-Americans live abroad. (Reuters)

Trump has often promoted policies that would allow Mexican-Americans to live in the U.S. without fear of "extreme vetting," a process that his administration calls "extreme vetting" because immigrants will be "domiciled and, ultimately, held in check."

Separately, Trump's march to the U.S. presidency will be a reminder of how he can turn conflicts over to foreign leaders.

In November 2016, Trump met with a Chinese-American judge and a Russian ally to discuss a U.S.-Russian visa program and export controls, the Department of Homeland Security said. The meeting also represented a major effort by Trump to mollify the Chinese government about the massive trade in goods from China brought into the U.S.

Domingo Seguin, a Democrat in the State Assembly, recently endorsed Trump for president, but said he never expected him to be a Republican outsider.

"I just don't think the fact that he wants to win will help him," Seguin said.

Dixit VELVERE, France (January 2, 2016) – The French Security Service (FSB) on Friday announced the identification of a suspect in the attack on a Paris cafe and attempted robbery on January 5 following a terrorist attack.

The suspect, named by French authorities as Kouachi Kouachi, appeared at the offices of the Paris-based al-Qaeda branch which was involved in the reported attack. Two P17-B Kalashnikov assault rifles, two pistols, a knife and a grenade launcher were also recovered from the scene. Police have named the suspect as Mohamed Brahimi from Talépous, outside Paris, after his brother Brahimi was killed by a suicide bomber, according to local media.

"The attack was not carried out by militants from ISIS or any other terrorist group. On January 5, 2015, a man attempted to enter the office of the satirical French satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo. The perpetrator of the attack fled to a nearby place of worship."

A statement from Paris' central police police, which was sent out to the site where the attack took place, confirmed a man had been arrested, but declined to give further details.

The attack began at the offices of Charlie Hebdo, along with the Charlie Hebdo Le Pen newspaper, before the shooting. La Charlie Neufchateau lost control of a restaurant and an employee in the attack.

After the assailant drove up to the Charlie Hebdo offices, as the newspaper initially appeared to, employees arrived to collect their food and grabbed the phones of staff and security officers.

Shortly before the attack, a man armed with a long knife was followed by a group of police officers, who stormed the offices.

Investigators believe that the attacker is an al-Qaeda member, or a lone gunman, who entered the offices from behind and began shooting at Charlie Hebdo staff.

Sources in the press circulated to the press that three people were killed as security forces allegedly tried to pick up two hostages when they were able to get into the restaurant.

Sources in the French media expect that the attack may have been a terrorist attack, but also that most of the names identified may also be of terrorists who could have suffered no loss of life while in France.

Paris-based French media sources also claimed that two other French citizens, both 21 years old, lived at the Montpellier residence they were staying with.

One source told AFP that French authorities were "working with" the "local authorities at Place de la Republique" to eventually identify those responsible for the attack on the site.

France has carried out an extensive operation to trace the whereabouts of large quantities of suspected terrorists crossing the French border, which includes the jihadists who carried out a number of attacks.

"We are being effectively targeted. The Paris attacks will go on," Paris police spokesman Bernard Cazeneuve told AFP during an interview.

The Charlie Hebdo massacre is not the first time France's "radical Islamist" past has been blamed on the Islamic State.

In January 2016, French security forces arrested a French-born Salafist jihadist who was thought to have planned to carry out a string of mass attacks.
