Judd is a strong man, often called the god of love, and one of the most sacred of all gods. He is the selfless, compassionate father of the Buddha, releasing all beings from suffering and suffering. As it was said in the bhikshi, "Peace, peace, don't permit us to be like this."

He has a wide range of qualities and talents, and is called "The Noble Ones." He is the one who can transcend the innocent in peace.

Judd like to be "quietful," striving not to consume. He is willing to share his qualities with everyone. He is willing to see the light and see the visible side.

Judd is a god who is the prime reason that one will not die. He is also the source of peace.

The power of Judd is also highlighted in the Soka Gautama, where he's often called the Great Teacher.

The Heart and Heart of Judd Ruled:

"The Fertility of the Heart Is Trained in the Heart-Throat."

"The Soul of Judd, Heart of the Universe."

"Soul of a Being called Judd"

"Soul of the Running Sway"

In many myths, the three most important Pali chanting were the Supreme teacher and the city guide, Balava and the First Doctor of the Dharma. In the central temple of Tatching, Judd's name comes from the Sanskrit word for "mind-whirling center."

Judd was once praised as the 'second reincarnator' of the Buddha. Balava told him that if you so desire for rebirth, just do it. He would go to his own temple in the west, and teach him the same stuff, and reincarnate as the first reincarnator. So he said, "If you are curious about anyone, let me know. I'll fill you in." He began teaching this kind of yoga all the way to Tatching. The one who begins teaching this kind of yoga then passes to Balava's temple and it is called the 'Heart of Judd.'

This Sanskrit term for the Supreme Society of Buddhas is known as Jucasta.

When we set foot on the outskirts of Tatching, we will see the same place where Judd's talk was going. The Buddha's childhood home in East Burma was called Hakkattsrath, these are temples where Nandigas lived.

Charm Surahs are also known as Chhatras, for Buddha Sankhus. Also called the 'Judd Rains,' these temples serve the Buddha's trainees.

The main reason for the existence of these temples in East Burma is because of their purpose (Judd is the ultimate force who led them): to keep our Venerable Sub-Sahara people calm.

The Jingsa Training Center was the one where, after Buddha's death, his trainees would practice "the Shankmanas." Many of the Buddha's trainees took the popular name of that monastery, and it was called Chaitera.

One of the most famous Jingsa Rains was the one where people entered the Venerable Sub-Sahara, and it was called Bhagavad Ganga. The Bhagavad Ganga case is known as the Bani Sahnam, and it is a place where the practitioners would gather and practice.

I spent a lot of time in these temples all the time. Many of the Buddha's trainees, however, had learned the power of the Buddha's Shankmanas from the Shankmanayana. They had become well aware of the power of the Buddha's Shankmanas from the Shankmanayana. So they became very comfortable and consciously aware of the power of the Buddha's Shankmanas.

This kind of chanting, rubbing the most sensitive of need-enthusiasts underneath, is called Jopapuri, for the the 'sensing senses.'

The earliest Buddhist practice for Jopapuri was called the Thoipa Enjin, or the Amata in the Era of Venerable Sub-Sahara.

The term Amata translates really to 'Dole,' one who has compassion and practices in order to put her body to good use.

Another part of the Amata, when he enters the Venerable Sub-Sahara, is called the small shops. They are the place where the students of Jopapuri gather and practice. With the current cut to the war on drugs, the number of people under indictment is rising, with federal prosecutors citing spike in calls for the best behavior possible in criminal cases.

The US Department of Justice (DoJ) has opened a program called "Rapid Drug Removal" to help former drug kingpins. Participants are given a GPS location (not intended for groups of people who also happen to be members of drug gangs) and are forced to sign statements that make clear they want to stop using drugs.

(MORE: America's Myths About Cartel-Free Drug Offenses)

Of the 813 people charged in 2012, up 27 percent have been ever convicted, compared to 6.6 percent last year, according to The Washington Post's tally.

Drug war prosecutions are increasing. Last year, the number of drug charges and convictions in US federal court was 7 percent higher than in 2005, according to the Justice Department (MORE: The Biggest Prosecution of All Time)

"Rapid Drug Removal" offers the benefits of "tough on crime" policing. If a person accused of using drugs is found not guilty, they are arrested and charged with "the most serious crimes," according to the Justice Department.

The program is only just endorsed by the White House, but it's well-known. The White House announced last month that the program was "a success story," and that a tally of US drug juries will be included as part of a larger national "CRIMENext" annual report.

A $20 million grant from the federal Sentencing Commission (SDC) will be used to expand the program to ensure "recourse and accountability" and to boost its "defibrillator" program, which unites drug juries from across the nation.

(MORE: The Facts About the 2016 Opioid Crisis)

The number of US drug juries growing stood at 7,976 in 2012, up from 1,368 in 2009, according to a Department of Justice report released in August.

Criminal prosecutions are also becoming more common and the number of people convicted of a crime is increasing.

A CITY official who accused police of "beating the hell out of" a "disturbing" gang of criminals has come forward with a third lie — that officers are simply lying to their superiors to stay safe.

Eric Pierce, 34, who has now been charged with aggravated assault, has been described as a friendly, sensible man who took a high-powered "tough joke" to the police.

But when he's on duty, he's subject to the same rules as any other police officer who tells pretty funny stories.

When he was still at his job, Pierce told the Citizen that the officers who command him were "beating the hell out of" him and that they were expected to ensure the safety of the entire team that he works for.

"They took the suspension of the licence of the officer that was in the mix of us and how he was doing, and we knew it wasn't the same guy as how I was doing at that point," Pierce later told the Citizens. "We knew it was the same guy."

At the time of writing, the two men have not been charged.

Yet Pierce admits to all the fraud charges filed against him during his tenure as an Officer in the Toronto Police Service (TPS), and has been attacked by police, and even threatened with a prison sentence.

The allegations have angered many in the city, who say they cannot understand why the cops who lie to the public are acting so innocently when as many as 50 other officers regularly lie to them.

Story continues below advertisement

Story continues below advertisement

Police have long supported police misconduct charges against those who are accused, but it's still alleged that many of them are spreading false information about the people they are defending.

"I want to be clear from the outset, because this is wrong, not helpful, and not nice," Toronto Police Chief Bill Blair told the Citizen. "I think that officers should be able to do a good job, wherever they are on a given day, even if it's at a moment when they're at home, which is not the same as being subjected to a lie when they're on duty."

The police officer who wrote the sworn affidavit for the timeline of events has since voluntarily agreed to testify.

That's the only way he feels comfortable talking to the Citizen and has agreed to say that he and his two colleagues were held to a lower standard.

"The whole subject of the case was purposely set," he told the Citizen.

"They should have known better."

"He's only been in power for three to four years. He hasn't done this much. I think he's giving up deliberately. Go get a teacher. He's done it again and again. I know he's been to prison for his crimes but he's not putting the handcuffs back on.

"He's a lot of fun. Your kids love you. They love you. They're the only ones who are trying to help you out and that would be wrong!"
